deaRegistrationStatus.headline: DEA Registration Status — Oklahoma Operators

tracks.status: Dispensary Portal CONFIRMED CLOSED June 22 — Portal closed June 22; official 60-day window still open through June 26–27; dispensaries that missed portal must contact DEA directly; 3 DAYS REMAIN to June 26; optional per OMMA
tracks.detail: The DEA Medical Marijuana Dispensary Registration Portal opened April 29, 2026 at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov. Fee $794/yr (PayPal only). OMMA states registration is OPTIONAL for dispensaries — non-registrants may continue operating under Oklahoma state law. JUNE 22 — PORTAL CONFIRMED CLOSED: The dedicated dispensary registration portal (mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov) closed June 22, 2026. Law firm Fennemore Craig confirmed the portal was 'Open Through June 22, 2026' and Foley Hoag LLP confirmed a 'hard stop to registrations on June 22, 2026.' The June 22 date also appears in the April 28 Federal Register notice (2026-08177) as the deadline for DEA to notify selected ALJ hearing participants — a confirmed, separate use of the same date. The dispensary portal is closed. Dispensaries that did not file by June 22 must contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before the official 60-day regulatory window closes June 26, 2026 (last business day, a Friday) / June 27 (last calendar day) — 3 days remain as of June 23. Vertically integrated operators also holding a manufacturer/distributor license remain subject to the OBNDD requirement below.

sources.name: OMMA — DEA application instructions for dispensaries
MJBizDaily — DEA registration is a tough choice (June 5, 2026)

tracks.status: No dedicated portal yet — OBNDD says required — Form 225 is only current path — 3 DAYS REMAIN to June 26
tracks.detail: As of 2026-06-23 the dedicated DEA application forms for Medical Marijuana Manufacturer, Bulk Manufacturer (cultivator/grower), Analytical Lab, and Distributor are NOT yet live. A DEA Diversion Control notice (~May 11, 2026) said forms are 'coming in the coming weeks' — now approximately six weeks later with the expedited window closing in 3 days (June 26, 2026), no dedicated portal has launched and it is certain one will not launch before the deadline. DEA Form 225 with medical-marijuana drug codes 7362 / 7353 / 7386 is the ONLY viable pathway to meet the expedited deadline. CRITICAL WARNING: Some operators read the 'coming soon' coverage and are waiting for the dedicated portal — this is a critical error with only 3 days remaining. Do not wait. Fees under Form 225: $3,699/yr (Manufacturer), $1,850/yr (Distributor), $296/yr (Analytical Lab). DEA has confirmed that operators who apply via Form 225 before the dedicated portal launches do NOT need to reapply — DEA will contact them to continue processing. OBNDD (May 8, 2026) states manufacturers and distributors are REQUIRED to obtain DEA registration; enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027.

deaRegistrationStatus.expeditedWindow: 60-day expedited window closes June 26, 2026 (last business day, Friday) / June 27 (last calendar day = 60 days from April 28 Federal Register publication). 3 DAYS REMAIN as of June 23 for manufacturers/cultivators via Form 225. DISPENSARY PORTAL CLOSED JUNE 22 (yesterday): The dedicated dispensary registration portal (mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov) closed June 22, 2026 per credible law firm sources (Fennemore Craig: 'Portal is Open Through June 22, 2026'; Foley Hoag LLP: 'hard stop to registrations on June 22, 2026'). Dispensaries that missed the portal must contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before the official 60-day window closes June 26–27. For manufacturers/cultivators using Form 225: the official deadline is June 26–27 — the dedicated manufacturer portal has not launched and will not launch before the deadline; use Form 225 now. D.C. Circuit: SAM/NDASA filed their opening brief June 4; a combined stay motion by SAM/NDASA/MMJ and co-petitioners was filed June 13, 2026; MMJ International filed a Statement of Issues seeking vacatur of the Final Order on June 18, 2026; Nebraska/Indiana opening brief due ~June 26; government (DOJ) response due July 2. No stay has been granted as of June 22, 2026. JUNE 18–19 DEVELOPMENT: DEA released the participant list for the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing — ALL selected participants oppose rescheduling: NDASA, Tennessee Bureau of Investigation, SAM, states of Nebraska/Idaho/Indiana/Louisiana, DUID Victim Voices, Kenneth Finn MD, and Phillip A. Drum PharmD — seven parties total, zero pro-reform. NORML and all cannabis industry groups were excluded. JUNE 19 DEVELOPMENT — CHIEF ALJ SCHEDULING ORDER: Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius signed a 12-page scheduling order on June 19, 2026 setting key prehearing deadlines: government notices of appearance due June 22; designated party prehearing statements (≤25 pages) due June 24; proposed exhibit exchange due June 24–25; ALJ to issue detailed hearing schedule by June 24. CRITICAL SCOPE CLARIFICATION: The order explicitly states the June 29 hearing does NOT address rescheduling of FDA-approved products or state-licensed medical marijuana — that 'has already occurred' on April 28 — and that 'the narrow issue in this matter is whether the remainder of marijuana, as defined in the CSA, should be transferred from schedule I to schedule III.' This does NOT affect Oklahoma operators' Schedule III status or OBNDD registration obligation. JUNE 19–20 DEVELOPMENT: DEA has begun on-site inspections at cannabis businesses that applied for Schedule III registration. The Mississippi Medical Marijuana Association reported two dispensary members visited by 5–6 DEA representatives for ~6 hours each; the visits were described as collaborative and inquisitive. Oklahoma operators who file registration should expect physical DEA site visits as part of the standard review process. NOTABLE JUNE 15–16 DEVELOPMENT: Cannabis-industry attorney Jason Adelstone (Harris Sliwoski LLP) published an analysis stating the rescheduling rule is 'more likely than not' to be stayed and reversed IF petitioners establish standing and the court reaches the merits — the largest public concession by a cannabis-friendly law firm to date of the litigation risk.
deaRegistrationStatus.caution: Multiple consultants and vendors are marketing paid 'DEA registration' services, some implying guaranteed or expedited approval. The portal is a direct-to-applicant system; the DEA makes all decisions and cites a six-month review period. For manufacturers/cultivators: the correct deadline is June 26–27 via Form 225 — only 3 days remain as of June 23. For dispensaries: the dedicated portal (mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov) CLOSED JUNE 22. Dispensaries that did not file must contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before June 26 — the official 60-day window remains open but the dedicated portal is closed. The application's Section 4 self-incrimination question warrants independent legal counsel before any owner/officer answers and files. Registration grants DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority — operators should assess this exposure with counsel. CRITICAL NEW RISK — 280E REFUND CLAWBACK: The DOJ sued cannabis MSO TerrAscend in May 2026 demanding return of $8.3M in 280E refunds received in 2024 — the first known federal clawback of a pre-rescheduling 280E refund claim. Oklahoma operators should NOT file amended returns for pre-2026 tax years without specialized counsel and should NOT count on retroactive 280E relief until IRS/Treasury issues formal written guidance. UNVETTED SOURCES: Content-marketing websites with no editorial credentials (HealthDataConsortium.org, WeedPress, hrzenden.com/Zen Den Co.) are appearing in search results alongside legitimate law-firm guidance — verify all compliance information against .gov primary sources only. VENDOR FEE ALERT: Some vendors mix their own service fees into DEA fee tables — the government's dispensary fee is $794/yr per DEA's own schedule, not higher; verify all fees against deadiversion.usdoj.gov directly. MANUFACTURER PORTAL: Do NOT wait for the dedicated manufacturer/cultivator portal — it has not launched and will not launch before the June 26–27 window; use Form 225 now. DEA HEARING PARTICIPANT SELECTION (June 18–19): DEA released the June 29 ALJ hearing participant list — ALL 7 selected participants oppose rescheduling; cannabis industry, patients, and reform groups excluded. This may strengthen D.C. Circuit petitioners' procedural arguments, but the April 28 Schedule III order remains in effect and Oklahoma operators' registration obligation is unchanged. ALJ SCOPE CLARIFICATION (JUNE 19): Chief ALJ Derek Julius's scheduling order confirms the June 29 hearing covers ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana (the 'remainder') — state-licensed medical marijuana rescheduling is already done and NOT subject to the hearing. This does NOT affect Oklahoma operators' Schedule III status, 280E relief, or OBNDD registration obligation. CONFIRMED JUNE 19–20: DEA HAS BEGUN ON-SITE INSPECTIONS at dispensaries that filed registration applications — two Mississippi dispensaries visited by 5–6 agents for ~6 hours; described as collaborative. Oklahoma operators who registered via the dispensary portal (before June 22) or are filing Form 225 (by June 26–27) should immediately ensure facilities, employee records (SSNs for all personnel with controlled-substance access), security systems, and chain-of-custody records are inspection-ready before filing.

officialSources.name: Federal Register — Rescheduling of Marijuana (state-licensed medical)
OMMA — monitoring DEA status & SAM lawsuit

keyDeadlines.label: Dispensary DEA Registration — Dedicated Portal CLOSED June 22; Official 60-Day Window Closes June 26–27
keyDeadlines.date: June 26–27, 2026 (official 60-day window) — URGENT; dedicated portal closed June 22
keyDeadlines.description: The dedicated dispensary registration portal (mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov) CLOSED June 22, 2026 per law firms Fennemore Craig and Foley Hoag LLP. The official 60-day expedited window under 21 CFR 1301.13(k) still closes June 26, 2026 (last business day, Friday) / June 27 (last calendar day) — 3 days remain as of June 23. Dispensaries that missed the portal must contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before June 26 to attempt registration within the official window. OMMA states dispensary registration is optional. The June 22 date in Federal Register document 2026-08177 also referred to DEA notifying selected ALJ hearing participants — a separate, confirmed use of the same date.

keyDeadlines.label: DEA Expedited 60-Day Registration Window
keyDeadlines.description: DEA expedited 60-day registration window closes. Apply within 60 days of the April 28 Federal Register publication to keep operating during the six-month DEA review. Only 3 days remain as of June 23. For cultivators, manufacturers, labs, and distributors: the dedicated DEA portal has NOT launched — use DEA Form 225 with drug codes 7362/7353/7386 NOW. Do not wait for the dedicated portal. NOTE: The dedicated dispensary portal closed June 22; dispensaries that missed must contact deadiversion.usdoj.gov directly before June 26.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Deadline — OMMA Manufacturers & Distributors
keyDeadlines.description: OBNDD deadline for OMMA manufacturers & distributors to file for DEA registration. Failure may result in OBNDD administrative sanctions including revocation of the OBNDD registration that underpins OMMA grower licenses. Use DEA Form 225 with medical marijuana drug codes — the dedicated manufacturer portal has not yet launched.

keyDeadlines.label: D.C. Circuit — Nebraska/Indiana Brief; Govt Response July 2
keyDeadlines.description: Nebraska and Indiana AGs' opening brief expected ~June 26 in D.C. Circuit consolidated cases (26-1106/1130/1136), challenging the DEA rescheduling order. SAM/NDASA filed their opening brief June 4. Government (DOJ) response to Nebraska/Indiana petition due July 2. A court ruling granting a stay would revert marijuana to Schedule I and nullify registrations filed under the expedited pathway. NDASA/MMJ stay motion (filed June 9) remains pending as of June 21. DEA released the June 29 ALJ hearing participant list June 18–19 — all 7 selected participants oppose rescheduling; zero pro-reform parties selected.

keyDeadlines.label: Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing
keyDeadlines.description: Broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing (DEA Arlington, VA). Scope: ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana — state-licensed medical marijuana rescheduling is already done and explicitly excluded per Chief ALJ Derek Julius's June 19 scheduling order. Determines whether remaining marijuana moves to Schedule III. July 3 recess, July 6 reconvene. All 7 selected participants oppose rescheduling — NORML and cannabis industry groups excluded. No pre-hearing stay has been granted as of June 21, 2026; hearing remains on schedule.

keyDeadlines.label: ALJ Hearing — Prehearing Statements & Exhibits Due
keyDeadlines.description: Per Chief ALJ Derek Julius's June 19 scheduling order, all 7 designated hearing parties must file prehearing statements (≤25 pages) and exchange proposed exhibits by June 24–25, 2026. The ALJ will also issue a detailed hearing schedule by June 24. Internal to the ALJ proceeding — no action required by Oklahoma operators — but signals the June 29 hearing is fully on track. The hearing covers ONLY adult-use marijuana; state-licensed medical marijuana rescheduling is already done.

keyDeadlines.label: Oklahoma New-License Moratorium Expiration (Extended to Aug 2028)
keyDeadlines.description: Oklahoma HB 3143 was signed into law by Governor Stitt on May 7, 2026, extending the new-license moratorium from August 1, 2026 to August 1, 2028. No new cultivation, processing, dispensary, or transport licenses will be issued until that date. Companion HB 3144 (2,500-license cultivation cap) passed the House; its enactment status is pending confirmation.

keyDeadlines.label: Federal Hemp Redefinition Takes Effect
keyDeadlines.description: Federal hemp redefinition takes effect: 0.3% total-THC threshold (includes THCA); final-product cap 0.4 mg THC; synthetics excluded. Delta-8 / Delta-10 / THCA-flower market effectively eliminated unless reformulated.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Enforcement Grace Period Ends
keyDeadlines.description: OBNDD enforcement grace period ends. Oklahoma operators without DEA registration by this date face active OBNDD administrative sanctions, regardless of whether they applied before the June 27 window.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Annual Registration Renewal Deadline
keyDeadlines.description: Annual OBNDD registration renewal deadline for Oklahoma cannabis operators. Failure to renew by October 31 (effective November 1) results in loss of Metrc seed-to-sale tracking access and required cessation of operations — independent of any DEA registration issue. This deadline is separate from and earlier than the OBNDD enforcement grace period for DEA registration (January 1, 2027). Oklahoma cultivators, processors, distributors, and dispensaries should submit renewal applications well before this date.

keyDeadlines.label: Oklahoma HB 4454 — Edibles Restrictions Take Effect
keyDeadlines.description: Oklahoma HB 4454 (signed Gov. Stitt May 7, 2026) takes effect November 1, 2026. Key restrictions: THC limited to 10mg per serving, 100mg per package (20mg per drink container); bans packaging shapes resembling humans, animals, cartoons, or commercially available candy; bans marketing that appeals to minors. Dispensaries and processors producing or selling edibles must reformulate or rebrand non-compliant products before this date.

items.headline: DEA Reschedules State Medical Marijuana & FDA-Approved Products to Schedule III
items.summary: Acting AG Todd Blanche issued a final order moving (1) marijuana in FDA-approved drug products and (2) marijuana under a qualifying state medical license from Schedule I to Schedule III, using treaty-implementation authority (21 U.S.C. 811(d)(1)) without traditional notice-and-comment. Recreational/adult-use, unlicensed marijuana, and synthetically derived THC remain Schedule I. A parallel expedited rulemaking on broader rescheduling was simultaneously initiated.
items.whyItMatters: Most consequential federal cannabis action in decades — creates new DEA registration duties, 280E tax relief, and treaty-compliance obligations for thousands of operators.

source.name: DOJ Press Release

items.headline: DEA Registration Now Required — Expedited Window Closes ~June 26–27, 2026
items.summary: Dispensary portal opened April 29, 2026 at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov ($794/yr, PayPal only) — OMMA states registration is OPTIONAL for dispensaries; the dedicated dispensary portal CLOSED June 22, 2026 per law firms Fennemore Craig and Foley Hoag LLP, 4 days before the 60-day Federal Register window closes June 26–27. Dedicated DEA application forms for Manufacturer, Bulk Manufacturer (cultivator/grower), Analytical Lab, and Distributor are NOT yet live as of 2026-06-22; a DEA Diversion Control notice (~May 11) said forms are 'coming in the coming weeks,' and the dedicated portal has not launched. The parallel pathway is DEA Form 225 with codes 7362/7353/7386 (fees: $3,699/yr manufacturer; $1,850/yr distributor; $296/yr lab). OBNDD states manufacturers and distributors are REQUIRED to register; enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027. The expedited 60-day window closes ~June 26–27 (60 days from April 28 Federal Register publication).
items.whyItMatters: Every cultivator, processor, lab, distributor, and dispensary faces a new federal registration layer. Dispensaries' dedicated portal CLOSED June 22 — dispensaries that missed must contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before June 26–27. Manufacturers/cultivators must use Form 225 now with 3 days remaining as of June 23 — OBNDD says non-registration risks sanctions. Missing the expedited window means operating without safe-harbor during a six-month DEA review.

source.name: Marijuana Moment

items.headline: OBNDD Mandates DEA Registration for OMMA Manufacturers & Distributors — Deadline ~June 27; Grace Period to Jan 1, 2027
items.summary: OBNDD Director Donnie Anderson directed that every OMMA registrant who is a "distributor or manufacturer" must obtain federal DEA registration. Applying within 60 days of the April 28 publication (~June 27, 2026) lets a business keep operating under state authority during DEA's six-month review. Anderson warned that failure "could result in OBNDD administrative sanctions up to and including the potential revocation" of OBNDD registrations, with an enforcement grace period running until January 1, 2027. The DEA application has seven sections and costs approximately $3,699 for manufacturers and $1,850 for distributors (via Form 225).
items.whyItMatters: OBNDD's requirement reaches ALL licensed manufacturers (growers) and distributors, not just dispensaries; growers who miss it risk losing the OBNDD manufacturer registration that underpins their OMMA grower license.

source.name: Marijuana Moment

items.headline: Oklahoma Moratorium Extended to 2028 — HB 3143 Signed May 7; HB 3144 Cultivation Cap Pending Confirmation
items.summary: Governor Kevin Stitt signed HB 3143 into law on May 7, 2026, extending Oklahoma's new-license moratorium from August 1, 2026 to August 1, 2028; the bill also prohibits transfer of any license currently under investigation. Companion HB 3144 — which would cap total cultivation licenses at 2,500 statewide — passed the House (82–15); its enactment status remains unconfirmed as of June 19, 2026. The Oklahoma legislature adjourned sine die on May 14, 2026, closing the 2026 session. At House passage Oklahoma had 2,164 active grows, 686 processors, 1,421 dispensaries, and 58 transporters. The moratorium applies only to new licenses; existing licensees in good standing may still open new locations or transfer licenses with OMMA approval.
items.whyItMatters: The two-year moratorium extension is now law — no new cultivation or other cannabis licenses will be issued until at least August 2028. Existing grow licenses become scarcer and more valuable as transferable assets. If HB 3144 was enacted before sine die adjournment, the 2,500 cap locks the total count near its current level.

source.name: MJBizDaily — Oklahoma extends moratorium

items.headline: Schedule III + Single Convention Activates Five New Compliance Burdens for Cultivators
items.summary: Because the rescheduling order is grounded in treaty-implementation authority, it increases — not reduces — cultivator compliance burdens: (1) SSNs for all personnel with controlled-substance access; (2) disclosure of upstream supplier DEA numbers; (3) DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority; (4) a crop purchase-and-resale mechanism where DEA serves as exclusive purchaser at a nominal price before crops can leave the facility; (5) parallel federal recordkeeping including quota reporting and import/export permits. The 2026 cultivator administrative fee is $113/kg on the 6,675 kg aggregate quota.
items.whyItMatters: Operators who assumed "rescheduling = deregulation" face a compliance shock; the DEA purchase-resale mechanism restructures the cultivation supply chain.

source.name: Cannabis Tech

items.headline: D.C. Circuit Cases 26-1106/1130/1136: SAM/NDASA Brief Filed June 4 — Nebraska/Indiana Brief Expected June 26; Govt Response July 2; Louisiana Withdrew
items.summary: Three consolidated petition sets challenge the April 28 order: (1) Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) + NDASA, filed May 4 — opening brief filed June 4; (2) AGs of Nebraska and Indiana, filed May 22 — opening brief expected ~June 26; government (DOJ) response to Nebraska/Indiana petition due July 2 (Louisiana initially co-filed but AG Liz Murrill sought dismissal May 29, leaving only Nebraska and Indiana); (3) a coalition of five 'aggrieved persons' including addiction-treatment organizations (New Directions) and others, filed May 28. Arguments include: exceeds AG authority, violates APA notice-and-comment, implicates the major questions doctrine, and relies on a possibly unconstitutional ALJ structure. All petitioner groups seek both a stay and full vacatur. On June 9, NDASA + MMJ International Holdings + MMJ BioPharma Cultivation + MMJ BioPharma Labs separately filed a formal stay motion in Case 26-1136. No stay has been granted as of June 19, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: A stay could freeze the entire order — reverting state medical marijuana to Schedule I and nullifying registration, 280E relief, and research expansion. The Nebraska/Indiana brief (~June 26) and government response (July 2) are the next immediate court milestones; any subsequent stay ruling is the key risk event for Oklahoma operators.

source.name: NORML

items.headline: NDASA + MMJ BioPharma File D.C. Circuit Stay Motion — First Formal Stay Request in Case 26-1136
items.summary: On June 9, 2026, the National Drug and Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA), MMJ International Holdings, MMJ BioPharma Cultivation, and MMJ BioPharma Labs jointly filed a motion in D.C. Circuit Case No. 26-1136 seeking a stay of Attorney General Order No. 6754-2026 (the April 28 medical-marijuana rescheduling order). The petitioners argue (1) the order bypassed required notice-and-comment rulemaking; (2) the AG lacked authority to act without completing the statutory DEA hearing; and (3) MMJ International/MMJ BioPharma — which invested more than $10 million and nearly a decade developing cannabinoid pharmaceuticals through FDA/DEA channels — suffer commercial harm from state-licensed marijuana operators now competing under the same Schedule III classification without equivalent safety review. Supporting the stay motion, Harvard Medical School neuroscientist Dr. Bertha K. Madras filed an expert declaration (also June 9) warning the D.C. Circuit that rescheduling poses 'substantial' and 'unacceptably high' public health risks — particularly for adolescents and unborn children — and that marijuana does not meet the standards required of a medicine. This is a distinct stay motion from the June 4 SAM/NDASA opening brief. Petitioners may also seek emergency relief before the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing. No court ruling has issued as of June 19, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first formal motion in Case 26-1136 asking the D.C. Circuit to actually halt the order. A granted stay would revert state medical marijuana to Schedule I, nullify all DEA registrations filed under the expedited pathway, and undo 280E relief. Oklahoma operators filing DEA registration before the June 26–27 window preserves the safe-harbor regardless of litigation outcome; the risk of not registering (OBNDD sanctions, license jeopardy) is immediate and certain, while stay risk is real but not yet realized.

source.name: Marijuana Herald — NDASA/MMJ Ask Court to Pause Rescheduling

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — Drug Testing Industry Asks Court to Pause

items.headline: Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing Set for June 29 – July 15, 2026 — Only Rescheduling Opponents Selected as Participants; Constitutional Challenge Active
items.summary: Alongside the partial rescheduling, DEA withdrew its stalled August 2024 proceeding and issued a new Notice of Hearing for an expedited ALJ proceeding on whether ALL marijuana — including adult-use — should move to Schedule III via notice-and-comment rulemaking. The hearing runs June 29 – July 15, 2026 (July 3 recess) at DEA's Arlington, VA facility. PARTICIPANT SELECTION (June 18–19): DEA released its participant list and ALL 7 selected parties oppose rescheduling: National Drug & Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA); Tennessee Bureau of Investigation; Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM); the states of Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana, and Louisiana; DUID Victim Voices; Kenneth Finn, MD; and Phillip A. Drum, PharmD. NORML and all cannabis industry, patient, and reform organizations were excluded — zero pro-reform parties selected. A pre-hearing constitutional challenge is also active: the DOJ formally admitted in MMJ BioPharma Cultivation Inc. v. Bondi (Feb. 27, 2025) that DEA ALJ removal protections are unconstitutional; challengers argue that under Axon v. FTC (2022), subjecting parties to proceedings before an unlawful tribunal is itself irreparable injury. MMJ International announced plans to seek emergency injunctive relief against the hearing. If MMJ files an emergency motion against the hearing, the D.C. Circuit could be forced to rule on the constitutional question within days. No injunction has been granted and the hearing remains on schedule as of June 19, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: The hearing's all-opponent participant roster means the administrative record will be built exclusively by rescheduling opponents — no commercial, patient, or consumer perspective included. This structural imbalance may strengthen D.C. Circuit petitioners' procedural arguments and increases the risk that the broader rescheduling recommendation will oppose Schedule III for adult-use marijuana. For Oklahoma operators, neither the hearing's outcome nor its participant composition affects the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order already in effect, nor does it change the June 26–27 registration deadline.

source.name: Gibson Dunn

secondarySource.name: Business of Cannabis — Mounting Legal Challenges

items.headline: DEA Names 7 Anti-Rescheduling Parties for June 29 ALJ Hearing — Zero Pro-Reform Parties Selected
items.summary: DEA released its participant list for the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing on approximately June 18–19, 2026, and every selected participant opposes marijuana rescheduling. The 7 selected parties are: National Drug & Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA); Tennessee Bureau of Investigation; Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM); the states of Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana, and Louisiana; DUID Victim Voices; Kenneth Finn, MD; and Phillip A. Drum, PharmD. NORML, cannabis industry organizations, patient advocates, and all reform groups that filed notices of intent to participate were excluded. The Federal Register notice had stated DEA would notify selected participants by June 22, 2026; notification went out approximately June 18–19. The hearing runs June 29 – July 15, 2026 at DEA's Arlington, VA facility and determines whether all forms of marijuana should move to Schedule III via notice-and-comment rulemaking.
items.whyItMatters: The administrative hearing record will be built exclusively by rescheduling opponents — no cannabis industry, patient, or consumer perspective will be represented. For Oklahoma operators, this has two implications: (1) the hearing is structured to produce an all-opposition record, likely recommending against Schedule III for adult-use marijuana; and (2) the exclusion of all pro-reform parties strengthens procedural arguments available to D.C. Circuit petitioners challenging the April 28 order, potentially increasing the risk of a court stay or vacatur. This hearing development does NOT affect the immediate June 26–27 registration deadline or OBNDD's mandatory requirement for Oklahoma cultivators and manufacturers.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — DEA Names 7 Anti-Rescheduling Parties, Zero Pro

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Picks Only Opponents for Hearing

items.headline: Chief ALJ Derek Julius Signs Scheduling Order — June 29 Hearing Explicitly Limited to Adult-Use 'Remainder'; State Medical Marijuana Already Done; Prehearing Deadlines June 24
items.summary: DEA Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius signed a 12-page scheduling order on June 19, 2026, setting the procedural roadmap for the June 29–July 15 broader-rescheduling hearing. Key provisions: (1) government attorneys must file notices of appearance by June 22; (2) designated parties must file prehearing statements (≤25 pages each) by June 24; (3) parties must exchange proposed exhibits by June 24–25; (4) the ALJ will issue a detailed hearing schedule by June 24; (5) the hearing recesses July 3 and reconvenes July 6. CRITICAL SCOPE CLARIFICATION: The order explicitly states 'the scope of this hearing is not to discuss the rescheduling of medical products approved by the Food and Drug Administration that contain marijuana and of medical marijuana products already regulated by the states, which has already occurred' and that 'the narrow issue in this matter is whether the remainder of marijuana, as defined in the CSA, should be transferred from its current place on schedule I of the list of controlled substances to schedule III.' The government carries the burden of proof as proponent of the rule.
items.whyItMatters: The ALJ's explicit scope language provides official confirmation that the June 29 hearing does not threaten the April 28 Schedule III status of Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana operators — that rescheduling is already done and is not at issue in the administrative proceeding. The hearing determines only whether adult-use/recreational marijuana also moves to Schedule III. For OMMA-licensed cultivators and dispensaries, this means the DEA registration obligation, 280E exemption, and all compliance burdens from the April 28 order are NOT on the line at this hearing. D.C. Circuit litigation remains a separate and real risk. Oklahoma operators' immediate priority remains filing DEA registration before June 22 (dispensaries) and June 26–27 via Form 225 (cultivators/manufacturers).

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Judge Issues Order Laying Out Process For Hearing This Month

items.headline: NORML Files Emergency Reconsideration After DEA Excludes All Pro-Reform Groups from June 29 ALJ Hearing
items.summary: Following the DEA's release of the June 29 hearing participant list — which includes only rescheduling opponents — NORML filed an emergency request for reconsideration with the DEA on approximately June 20, 2026, arguing that excluding all pro-reform parties from the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing violates the public interest. NORML contended that the exclusion 'would cause immediate and irreparable procedural prejudice, impair the completeness and balance of the administrative record, and disserve the public interest in a full and reasoned proceeding concerning the federal legal status of marijuana.' The American Trade Association for Cannabis and Hemp (ATACH) — also excluded — called the selection 'deeply disappointing' and stated it would continue advocacy through the Coalition for Cannabis Scheduling Reform. The DEA had already issued formal participant notifications on June 22; as of June 23, 2026, no public DEA response to NORML's reconsideration has been issued and the June 29 hearing remains on schedule with only the seven anti-rescheduling participants.
items.whyItMatters: NORML's emergency reconsideration confirms that the all-opponent participant roster is being formally challenged through administrative channels, not just commentary. If the DEA denies reconsideration — the likely outcome given notifications already issued — NORML and excluded industry groups may escalate to the D.C. Circuit, adding new procedural claims to the consolidated rescheduling challenges (26-1106/1130/1136). For Oklahoma operators, this does NOT affect the already-effective Schedule III status for state-licensed medical marijuana (Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order explicitly excludes that from the hearing's scope). The June 29 hearing and its one-sided record remain a risk factor for the future of broader adult-use rescheduling only. Oklahoma operators' immediate priority remains filing DEA registration via Form 225 before June 26–27 — 3 days remain as of June 23.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — NORML Asks DEA To Reconsider Its Exclusion from Hearing

secondarySource.name: NORML — DEA Limits Participants in Hearing to Those Opposed (June 19, 2026)

items.headline: 280E Tax Deduction Bar Lifted for Medical Operators — Binding IRS/Treasury Guidance Still Pending
items.summary: State-licensed medical marijuana is no longer Schedule I/II, so operators are no longer subject to the IRC Section 280E deduction disallowance. Treasury/IRS announced April 23, 2026 that formal guidance is forthcoming and expected to treat rescheduling as applying for the full 2026 tax year. As of June 19, 2026 — over seven weeks after the order took effect — no revenue ruling, IRS notice, proposed regulation, or other binding written guidance has been published. Seven House Democrats formally demanded IRS and Treasury expedite guidance on May 28 (see companion item). No retroactive relief for prior years has been announced; recreational-only operators remain fully subject to 280E.
items.whyItMatters: Potentially tens of millions in aggregate annual tax savings for medical operators; however, operators and CPAs are acting on legal interpretation alone until IRS publishes binding guidance. Allocation methodologies for mixed-use operators and retroactivity scope remain unresolved. A May 2026 DOJ lawsuit demanding return of a cannabis MSO's pre-rescheduling 280E refund signals IRS will aggressively contest any pre-2026 retroactive claims.

source.name: U.S. Treasury

items.headline: IRS/Treasury 280E Guidance Still Not Issued — Seven House Democrats Demand Action
items.summary: Seven House Democrats led by Reps. Steven Horsford and Steve Cohen wrote to IRS Commissioner Danny Bisignano and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on May 28, 2026, demanding prompt publication of formal 280E guidance following the April 28 medical-marijuana rescheduling order. As of June 19, 2026 — over seven weeks after rescheduling took effect — neither IRS nor Treasury has published a revenue ruling, IRS notice, proposed regulation, or other binding written guidance on how 280E applies to Schedule III medical-marijuana operators. The April 23 Treasury press release (SB0471) announced intent to issue guidance including a transition rule treating relief as applying for the full 2026 taxable year; it is not itself binding. Operators and CPAs are acting on the legal interpretation that 280E does not apply to state-licensed medical activity, but without IRS confirmation of allocation methodologies or treatment of mixed-use operators.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma medical-marijuana operators — cultivators, processors, and dispensaries — cannot finalize 2026 tax strategy with certainty until IRS publishes formal guidance. The seven-week gap between the rescheduling order and any binding IRS action leaves operators exposed if Treasury ultimately limits relief in ways not currently anticipated.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — 7 House Democrats Demand 280E Guidance

secondarySource.name: The Marijuana Herald — Lawmakers Urge IRS and Treasury

items.headline: DOJ Sues TerrAscend for $8.3M in First Known 280E Refund Clawback — IRS Signals Pre-2026 Claims Will Be Contested
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Justice filed suit May 18, 2026 in U.S. District Court in New Jersey seeking $8.3 million plus interest from cannabis MSO TerrAscend USA — the first known instance of the federal government attempting to recover a 280E tax refund proactively claimed by a cannabis operator. TerrAscend filed an amended 2020 tax return in April 2024 claiming $64.2 million in deductions not permitted under Section 280E, and the IRS sent an $8.3M refund check in June 2024. The DOJ complaint alleges the refund was 'erroneous' because IRS's Joint Committee on Taxation did not review it as required and because TerrAscend was not entitled to the deductions. An IRS bulletin issued the same month TerrAscend received the refund had already warned that amended returns seeking 280E relief were 'not entitled to a refund or payment.' TerrAscend deposited the check the same day it arrived and has 'not voluntarily returned' the funds. Across publicly traded cannabis MSOs, total disputed 280E claims total approximately $1.6 billion.
items.whyItMatters: IRS and DOJ have drawn a clear line: pre-rescheduling 280E refund claims for tax years before 2026 will be aggressively contested, not honored. Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators considering amended returns for 2024, 2023, or earlier years face the same enforcement risk. The April 28 rescheduling order eliminates 280E going forward (January 1, 2026 and after, per expected Treasury guidance), but IRS has signaled it will fight retroactive relief for prior years without formal written authorization — which has still not been published as of June 19, 2026. Do NOT file pre-2026 amended returns without specialized tax counsel.

source.name: Business of Cannabis — DOJ Sues TerrAscend for $8.3M in First Known 280E Refund Clawback

secondarySource.name: MJBizDaily — IRS: Cannabis MSO must return 'erroneous' 280E refund

items.headline: Congress Closes the Hemp Loophole — Delta-8 / THCA / Synthetics Banned Effective Nov 12, 2026
items.summary: The Continuing Appropriations and Extensions Act, 2026 (H.R. 5371 §781), enacted November 12, 2025, rewrote the federal hemp definition: the 0.3% THC threshold now counts total THC including THCA; final consumable products are capped at 0.4 mg total THC per container; intermediates are capped at 0.3% total THC dry-weight post-decarboxylation; cannabinoids not naturally produced by the plant or synthesized outside it are excluded and treated as Schedule I. Products exceeding thresholds after November 12, 2026 revert to Schedule I.
items.whyItMatters: Functionally eliminates the current Delta-8/Delta-10/THCA-flower intoxicating-hemp market unless reformulated or Congress acts before the effective date.

source.name: Arnold & Porter Advisory

items.headline: OMMA Monitors Rescheduling & Confirms 280E Relief Applies Regardless of DEA Registration Status
items.summary: OMMA's official notice confirms it is monitoring DEA rescheduling, that approximately 4,000 OMMA licensees are affected, and that 280E removal applies to Oklahoma medical operators regardless of whether a licensee pursues DEA registration. OMMA cautioned that all information "remains subject to change pending petition outcomes" — a reference to the consolidated D.C. Circuit litigation.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma operators get federal tax relief now, but the pending lawsuits are the key wildcard that could reverse it.

source.name: OMMA Official Notice

items.headline: Oklahoma Grower License: Current Compliance Stack — Metrc, OBNDD Registration, 75% Residency, $50K Bond
items.summary: Commercial growers must comply with OMMA rules and hold an OBNDD "Manufacturer" registration. Key requirements: ≥75% Oklahoma-resident ownership; owners ≥25 years old; background checks (nonviolent felony within 2 yrs / any felony within 5 yrs disqualifying); $50,000 surety bond or 5+ years land ownership; not within 1,000 ft of a school; outdoor growers register with the Oklahoma Department of Agriculture; active Metrc seed-to-sale tracking (tags at vegetative stage, movements reported within 24 hrs, sales pushed to Metrc same day). Tier-1 fees range $2,558.30 to $50,000+ by canopy size.
items.whyItMatters: The 75% residency rule is the main anti-foreign-ownership mechanism; post-moratorium applicants will face all of this plus the new DEA registration layer.

source.name: OMMA Grower License

items.headline: Illegal-Grow Crackdown: 51-Defendant Federal Indictment; 1.7M+ Plants Seized Since 2021
items.summary: A multi-agency task force (Oklahoma AG Organized Crime Task Force, OBN/OBNDD, OMMA, ICE/HSI, Cherokee Nation Marshals, National Guard) targets illegal grows, many linked to Chinese nationals. A June 24, 2025 Craig/Mayes County operation seized 40,723 plants and 1,000+ lbs of processed marijuana. In April 2026 a DEA-led Homeland Security Task Force announced a 51-defendant federal indictment over a nationwide black-market conspiracy originating in Oklahoma. OBNDD Director Anderson testified that product from ~85 million plants is unaccounted for. Since 2021: 1.7 million illegal plants seized, 181,000 lbs confiscated, 302 arrests.
items.whyItMatters: Licensed growers compete against massive illegal supply, and OMMA agents are embedded in the criminal task force — administrative investigative powers are being used in prosecutions.

source.name: DEA Press Release

items.headline: OMMA New Testing Laboratory Rules Take Effect June 1, 2026
items.summary: OMMA's updated permanent testing laboratory rules took effect June 1, 2026, the product of a multi-year process directed by HB 4056 (2022) in which seven licensed lab directors met across 15 sessions (90+ hours) and submitted 120 pages of recommendations. The rules amend OAC 442:10-8-1 and OAC 442:10-8-3 and address manufacturer's instructions for use, enrichment steps and quality control sample requirements, inventory tag requirements, and clarifications on yeast/mold and potency-confirmation testing for distillate. A prior set of emergency rule updates for licensed laboratories was approved separately. Stakeholders at the public comment hearing had pressed OMMA for clearer standards and broader lab access to Metrc data.
items.whyItMatters: Cultivators and processors submitting products for third-party testing must now meet the updated standards. Products that fail under new methodology cannot legally enter the distribution chain. Labs out of compliance with the new equipment or procedural rules face potential OMMA enforcement, which could create bottlenecks affecting product release timelines for growers and manufacturers.

source.name: OMMA — New Testing Lab Rules Effective June 1

items.headline: Oklahoma Cannabis Market in Freefall — Wholesale Prices Down 63%, 6,000+ Grows Shuttered
items.summary: Oklahoma Watch and KGOU (NPR Oklahoma) reported June 2, 2026 that the state's medical cannabis market is in severe contraction. Wholesale prices have fallen from approximately $3,000/lb at peak to $1,100/lb (≈63% decline), making Oklahoma the national price floor at $2.11/gram. A 2023 study found licensed supply exceeded patient demand by roughly 64 to 1. At peak, Oklahoma had nearly 14,000 licensed businesses including ~9,400 growers; more than 6,000 grow locations have since shut down or been abandoned. The industry generated $670M+ in retail sales and $1.1B+ in wholesale transactions in 2025, contributing ~$47M in excise taxes and $60M+ in state and local sales taxes — but revenue and margins are collapsing. Governor Stitt is calling for voters to shut the program down entirely; legislative leadership has resisted abolition.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and processors face existential margin pressure from oversupply and falling wholesale prices at the same moment new DEA registration costs ($3,699/yr for manufacturers) are being imposed. Dispensary customers face the same squeeze from the retail side. The license moratorium extension (HB 3143, signed May 2026) prevents new entrants but does nothing to reduce the existing licensed supply overhang. CAKE and its wholesale dispensary customers operate in this environment — the cost and compliance burden of DEA registration compounds an already-stressed market.

source.name: Oklahoma Watch — Growers Say Cannabis Bonanza Is Going to Pot

items.headline: OMMA Mandatory Recall — All Products Tested by Greenleaf Labs LLC Between April 2023 and July 2025 (~19,000 Samples)
items.summary: OMMA issued a mandatory recall of all medical marijuana products tested by Greenleaf Labs LLC (license LAAA-MP4O-T1EE) during April 2023 through July 2025, after finding the laboratory inaccurately calculated yeast and mold content throughout that period. Approximately 19,000 samples across multiple Oklahoma licensees are affected — among the largest lab-testing recalls by sample count in Oklahoma OMMA history. Operators whose products were tested by Greenleaf Labs during the affected window must identify, quarantine, and address affected inventory per OMMA recall procedures. Item date is approximate; confirm the exact recall issue date and current scope at the OMMA source URL.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and processors who used Greenleaf Labs for testing between April 2023 and July 2025 face mandatory recall obligations covering years of historical product. CAKE should immediately audit its testing records against Greenleaf Labs' license number (LAAA-MP4O-T1EE) — any product in that scope cannot legally be sold and must be quarantined. The recall follows the June 1, 2026 effective date of OMMA's new lab testing rules and reflects an elevated OMMA enforcement posture on testing integrity.

source.name: OMMA — Mandatory Recall: Greenleaf Labs LLC

items.headline: Oklahoma SQ 837 (Adult-Use Legalization Initiative) Will Not Appear on November 2026 Ballot — Petitioners Failed to Gather Signatures
items.summary: Proponents of Oklahoma State Question 837 — a citizen initiative that would have legalized adult-use recreational marijuana in Oklahoma — failed to gather the required petition signatures by the filing deadline. SQ 837 will not appear on the November 2026 ballot. Oklahoma voters previously rejected SQ 820 (adult-use legalization) in March 2023. With SQ 837 confirmed as non-qualifying, Oklahoma's cannabis market will remain medical-only through at least the 2028 election cycle barring a legislative referral.
items.whyItMatters: For CAKE and its wholesale dispensary customers, the failure of SQ 837 removes the only near-term market-expansion catalyst — adult-use legalization would have substantially broadened the consumer base and potentially relieved oversupply pressure. Oklahoma will remain a medical-only state for the foreseeable future. Business plans premised on near-term adult-use expansion in Oklahoma should not be advanced based on a 2026 ballot measure; the next realistic opportunity would be a 2028 initiative campaign.

source.name: Ballotpedia — Oklahoma State Question 837

items.headline: DEA Begins On-Site Inspections at Cannabis Businesses That Registered Under Schedule III
items.summary: The Drug Enforcement Administration began conducting on-site inspections at state-licensed cannabis businesses that applied for federal DEA registration following the April 28 rescheduling order. The Mississippi Medical Marijuana Association reported that two of its dispensary members were among the first in the country visited after submitting applications through the DEA Medical Marijuana Dispensary Registration Portal. High Street Dispensary in Jackson, MS (owner David Fowler) hosted 5–6 DEA representatives for approximately six hours; Fowler described the agents as 'very cordial' and 'did not come in overbearing,' noting the agents said they were 'new to this whole process too' and approached the visit as collaborative and inquisitive. Both visited dispensaries were told they were among the first in the country to receive inspections. DEA inspections are a standard step in processing new Schedule III registration applications — agents verify facility compliance, security, records, and personnel information provided in the application.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first confirmed ground-level evidence that DEA's Schedule III oversight is now operational — registration is not a paper exercise but triggers physical federal presence at the facility. Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators/manufacturers who file registration applications (dispensary portal by June 22; Form 225 by June 26–27) should expect an in-person DEA visit as part of the review process. Before filing, operators must ensure their facilities, employee documentation (SSNs for all personnel with controlled-substance access), security systems, record-keeping, and chain-of-custody logs are in order. The collaborative tone at these first visits is consistent with DEA's standard registration process — Schedule III inspections under 21 U.S.C. 880 include authority to enter at any time without advance notice. The inspection news does not change OBNDD's mandatory registration directive for manufacturers and distributors; it does mean non-registration avoidance strategies based on 'avoiding DEA visibility' are now tested against reality.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Begins On-Site Inspections at Marijuana Businesses

items.headline: Oklahoma HB 4454 Signed — Edibles Restricted to 10mg/Serving, 100mg/Package; Cartoon Shapes and Minor-Targeted Marketing Banned; Effective November 1, 2026
items.summary: Governor Kevin Stitt signed HB 4454 into law on May 7, 2026, effective November 1, 2026. The law limits THC in medical marijuana edibles to 10mg per serving and 100mg per package (20mg per drink container); bans packaging in shapes resembling humans, animals, cartoons, or commercially available candy; and prohibits marketing that appeals to minors. The bill was signed the same day as HB 3143 (license moratorium extension to August 2028).
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries must audit their entire edibles inventory for compliance before November 1, 2026 — approximately 4.5 months away. Processors and manufacturers producing edibles for the Oklahoma market must reformulate products exceeding 10mg/serving and redesign any non-compliant packaging before that date. Products in licensed inventory after November 1 that exceed the per-serving or per-package THC caps, or feature banned shapes, are out of compliance. Given OMMA's active enforcement posture (6,000+ inspections, 704 products recalled), this is an enforcement-ready standard. Dispensary buyers should flag non-compliant edible SKUs for reformulation or discontinuation now.

source.name: BillTrack50 — HB 4454 Oklahoma 2026

discourse.headline: Consultants marketing paid 'DEA registration' services
discourse.claim: Consulting firms and law practices are selling paid services to prepare/file DEA Schedule III registrations, framing the 7-section form as too complex to self-file; some imply a single SOP error will delay federal approval.
discourse.caution: The portal is direct-to-applicant with a $794 fee paid to DEA; there is no official statement that paid representation is required. Counsel may be prudent for the Section 4 liability question, but be skeptical of vendors implying only they can navigate a self-registration process.

source.name: CannDelta

discourse.headline: Section 4 'have you distributed without DEA registration?' debate
discourse.claim: The application asks whether owners/operators previously manufactured/distributed a controlled substance without DEA registration — which nearly every existing cannabis operator must answer 'yes' to. DEA publicly said the question is 'not intended as a categorical barrier.'
discourse.caution: DEA's reassurance is informal — not published policy or rulemaking, not binding. Former federal prosecutors have warned against treating it as legal protection. Independent counsel before submitting is essential.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times

discourse.headline: June 22 deadline confusion: WRONG for manufacturers/cultivators; NOW CONFIRMED as dispensary portal closing date by credible sources
discourse.claim: RESOLVED AS OF JUNE 22: The June 22 date is now confirmed as the dispensary portal's own administrative closing date. Fennemore Craig ('Portal is Open Through June 22, 2026') and Foley Hoag LLP ('hard stop to registrations on June 22, 2026') both confirmed this. The June 22 date in Federal Register document 2026-08177 also referred to DEA notifying selected ALJ hearing participants — a separate use confirmed June 18–19. For manufacturers and cultivators using Form 225, the correct operative deadline is June 26 (last business day) / June 27 (last calendar day) — 3 days remain as of June 23. Previously, unvetted sites (HealthDataConsortium.org, hrzenden.com/Zen Den Co.) published 'June 22' as a universal deadline — they were wrong about scope but the date itself was correct for dispensaries.
discourse.caution: June 22 is confirmed as the closing date for the dedicated DEA dispensary portal — this has now occurred. The general 60-day regulatory window under 21 CFR 1301.13(k) is June 26–27 (not June 22) and remains open for manufacturers/cultivators via Form 225. For CAKE as a cultivator/manufacturer, the operative deadline is June 26–27 via Form 225 — 3 days remain as of June 23. Dispensaries that missed the June 22 portal should contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before June 26. The June 22 confusion is now moot for dispensaries; the urgent live question is whether missed-portal dispensaries can still register via an alternative DEA pathway before June 26.

source.name: Fennemore Craig — 'Portal is Open Through June 22, 2026'

discourse.headline: '280E relief applies whether or not you register with DEA'
discourse.claim: OMMA guidance and several sources assert 280E relief applies to all state-licensed medical operators by virtue of Schedule III, regardless of DEA registration — used by some to justify skipping registration.
discourse.caution: IRS/Treasury has not issued formal guidance; OMMA's statement is its interpretation, not a binding ruling. Some tax attorneys dispute it. Acting on it before Treasury guidance carries material tax risk. The DOJ's May 2026 lawsuit against TerrAscend demanding return of a pre-rescheduling 280E refund underscores IRS's aggressive posture on any 280E claims it views as unsupported.

source.name: OMMA notice / Marijuana Policy Project Q&A

discourse.headline: Claims the whole rescheduling/registration framework could be voided
discourse.claim: The SAM/NDASA D.C. Circuit petition, if it gets a stay, could void the Final Order and potentially unwind registrations filed under it; commentators warn operators are 'racing through a door that could be slammed shut.'
discourse.caution: Active litigation — OMMA itself warned all information 'remains subject to change pending the lawsuit's outcome.' Factor this into any large federal-compliance capital spend.

source.name: OMMA — SAM lawsuit update

discourse.headline: Vendors implying guaranteed or expedited DEA approvals
discourse.claim: Some marketing implies engaging a firm ensures approval or prevents delays; a Newswire PR item touts a vendor as having 'the answer' to DEA's registration questions.
discourse.caution: DEA makes registration decisions; no third party can guarantee outcomes or speed DEA's stated six-month review. Guarantees of approval or 'special access' are red flags. Paid PR is not verified reporting.

source.name: Newswire (paid PR)

discourse.headline: 'DEA registration unlocks interstate commerce' — now being promoted by named companies via press release
discourse.claim: Some consultants suggest DEA registration will enable interstate cannabis commerce. This claim escalated in May 2026 when LEEF Brands (a California cannabis company) issued a May 8, 2026 press release titled 'LEEF Brands Files Applications for DEA Registration, Positioning for Interstate and Global Export Opportunities,' framing registration as the gateway to selling across state lines and exporting internationally. The release was picked up by Cannabis Business Times.
discourse.caution: Interstate cannabis commerce is not authorized by the Final Order, the Federal Register rules, or any federal guidance. The DOJ order explicitly states registered operators remain 'subject to the limitations of their state license.' The treaty conflict with the 1961 Single Convention further blocks export pathways. LEEF Brands' release uses future-conditional language ('as regulatory frameworks evolve'), but the headline framing has been treated as current fact in trade coverage. Do not make Oklahoma business decisions premised on interstate commerce unlocking via DEA registration.

source.name: LEEF Brands / GlobeNewswire (May 8, 2026)

discourse.headline: Oklahoma operators confused over OMMA 'optional' vs OBNDD 'required'
discourse.claim: Local Oklahoma coverage and industry media show operators unsure whether to register now or wait for the manufacturer portal, and confused that OMMA calls dispensary registration optional while OBNDD calls manufacturer/distributor registration mandatory.
discourse.caution: These are different agencies governing different license types — not contradictory. Industry media is not a substitute for counsel. Much of the live conversation is in private Facebook/Slack operator groups not publicly indexed.

source.name: KXII (Oklahoma) coverage

discourse.headline: Operators warn: DEA registration is 'giving DEA a roadmap' to your operation
discourse.claim: Some industry veterans argue DEA registration creates permanent operational exposure beyond the Section 4 self-incrimination question — it documents facility location, scale, personnel with controlled-substance access, and supply-chain partners, and grants DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority. The concern is that previously extralegal operations are now fully mapped for federal enforcement.
discourse.caution: DEA's on-demand inspection authority is a statutory consequence of Schedule III registration under 21 U.S.C. 880, not unique to cannabis. Operators who fear this 'roadmap' risk should weigh it against the concrete risks of non-registration: OBNDD sanctions, loss of expedited safe-harbor, and potential license jeopardy. This is a legitimate risk factor for counsel to evaluate — not a verified basis for refusing to register.

source.name: Beard Bros Pharms — Oklahoma registration concerns

discourse.headline: MMJ International press campaign claims June 29 hearing unconstitutional — 'DELAYED AGAIN!'; hearing remains on schedule with all-opponent participant list released
discourse.claim: Since May 18, 2026, MMJ International Holdings has run an escalating public press campaign with alarming headlines: 'DELAYED AGAIN! Will Marijuana Rescheduling Survive Constitutional Challenges?' (May 26); 'DEA's Last Marijuana Gamble: Unconstitutional ALJ System Collapses'; and others via AccessNewswire and pressrelease.com. MMJ formally joined the D.C. Circuit challenge (cases 26-1106/1130/1136) on May 29, and on June 9 co-filed the stay motion. MMJ has also announced plans to seek emergency injunctive relief against the June 29 hearing itself on constitutional grounds (DOJ admitted DEA ALJs' removal protections are unconstitutional in MMJ BioPharma v. Bondi, Feb. 27, 2025). UPDATE June 18–19: DEA released the June 29 hearing participant list — all 7 selected participants oppose rescheduling; NORML and cannabis industry excluded. No court has granted any injunction or stay, and the hearing remains on schedule as of June 19, 2026.
discourse.caution: MMJ International is an interested litigation party — a pharmaceutical company that spent 7+ years pursuing FDA/DEA approval channels and now competes against newly-Schedule-III state-licensed operators. Its press campaign is advocacy, not journalism. The constitutional issue over DEA ALJ removal protections is real (DOJ conceded it), but it primarily affects the broader rescheduling hearing, not the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order already in effect. Treat any claim that the June 29 hearing 'has been cancelled' or 'will definitely not proceed' as unverified until confirmed by the DEA or a federal court order. The risk of Oklahoma cultivators/processors not registering before June 26–27 due to MMJ's 'framework may be voided' narrative is concrete and immediate; the stay risk, while real, has not materialized.

source.name: PharmiWeb — June 29 Hearing Faces Constitutional Issues (May 18, 2026)

discourse.headline: Former federal prosecutor and IRS attorney warn: Do Not Rush DEA Registration
discourse.claim: A former federal prosecutor and a former IRS attorney are publicly advising cannabis operators NOT to file DEA registration before the June 27 expedited deadline without first consulting specialized criminal-defense and tax counsel. Arguments: (1) the Section 4 prior-distribution question documents conduct that was federally illegal when it occurred, and DEA's informal 'not a categorical barrier' statement is not binding policy; (2) the D.C. Circuit challenges could stay or vacate the entire order, making rushed registrations moot; (3) for operators whose primary concern is state-license continuity rather than federal supply-chain expansion, the registration risk may outweigh the benefit.
discourse.caution: This advice conflicts with OBNDD Director Anderson's directive that ALL Oklahoma OMMA manufacturers and distributors MUST register by ~June 27 to avoid OBNDD administrative sanctions up to license revocation. Unlike dispensaries (for whom OMMA calls registration optional), Oklahoma cultivators and processors face a state-level mandate from OBNDD — not merely a federal opportunity. Operators following this national commentary without accounting for Oklahoma's OBNDD requirement risk losing their OBNDD manufacturer registration and by extension their OMMA grower/processor license. Consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel familiar with both DEA registration and OBNDD requirements before deciding.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — Former Prosecutors Warn Against Rushing

discourse.headline: 'Federal rescheduling rewards a cannabis supply chain built on fraudulent safety testing' — MMJ International press campaign
discourse.claim: MMJ International Holdings (press release via AccessNewswire, June 3, 2026) claims the April 28 rescheduling order granted Schedule III status to a supply chain characterized by 'documented lab fraud, contaminated products, and a near-total absence of safety science' — without requiring a single state-licensed product to demonstrate safety, purity, or clinical efficacy. The company calls on federal courts and Congress to scrutinize the state-licensed supply chain before extending federal legitimacy. MMJ International is simultaneously a D.C. Circuit petitioner in Case 26-1136 and a co-filer of the June 9 stay motion.
discourse.caution: MMJ International Holdings is an interested litigation party — this is paid-wire advocacy, not independent journalism. Oklahoma has had documented lab-fraud cases (OMMA has acted on testing violations and issued product recalls involving fraudulent or failed lab results), so the underlying concern about testing integrity is not fabricated; however, the framing is designed to advance MMJ International's litigation strategy and commercial interests. As of June 19, no court has accepted this argument as grounds for a stay or vacatur. Oklahoma operators should monitor whether this argument gains traction in the D.C. Circuit, as a ruling that imposed additional safety-testing conditions on DEA registration would directly affect cultivators and manufacturers.

source.name: AccessNewswire — MMJ International Holdings (June 3, 2026)

discourse.headline: Unvetted content-marketing websites publishing authoritative-sounding but unreliable DEA compliance guidance
discourse.claim: Multiple websites with no identifiable editorial standards, named authors, or organizational credentials are publishing DEA registration guidance that appears in Google results alongside legitimate law-firm content: HealthDataConsortium.org ('June 22, 2026: What Medical Cannabis Operators Must Do Now' — no author, no organizational affiliation found); hrzenden.com / Zen Den Co. ('DEA Registration Deadline Cannabis June 22' — apparent lead-generation content from a non-cannabis business); WeedPress 'The Paper Trail' (aggressive compliance-cliff framing, operated by an individual, targeting South Dakota operators but indexed broadly). These sites are amplifying the June 22 deadline confusion and creating additional noise for operators seeking reliable guidance.
discourse.caution: Oklahoma operators searching for DEA registration guidance are encountering these sites in search results, sometimes above credible law-firm or government content. Their articles may contain factual errors, be out of date, or be designed primarily for lead generation rather than compliance accuracy. No website is an authoritative source unless it ends in .gov. Cross-check any guidance against primary sources: the Federal Register notice (federalregister.gov/documents/2026/04/28/2026-08177), the DEA Diversion Control page (deadiversion.usdoj.gov), OMMA official bulletins (oklahoma.gov/omma), and OBNDD's director bulletin (obndd.ok.gov).

source.name: HealthDataConsortium.org — 'June 22, 2026: What Medical Cannabis Operators Must Do Now'

discourse.headline: 'Fake Marijuana News' — MMJ International claims cannabis media is misleading investors about D.C. Circuit strength
discourse.claim: MMJ International Holdings issued two closely related press releases on June 12–13, 2026. The June 12 release was titled 'Marijuana Rescheduling Delayed or Denied? Marijuana Faces its Biggest Threat Yet: the Constitution' (HealthTechnologyNet). On June 13, 2026, MMJ issued a follow-up via AccessNewswire titled 'Fake Marijuana News: What the Cannabis Media Isn't Telling Investors About the D.C. Circuit Rescheduling Challenge.' MMJ alleges mainstream cannabis media (described as financially entangled with the cannabis industry) is downplaying the legal strength of the court challenges — specifically: (1) the petitioner coalition spans states (Nebraska, Indiana), national advocacy groups (SAM, NDASA), and pharmaceutical developers (MMJ International), giving it strong standing; (2) the DOJ itself has conceded that DEA ALJ removal protections are unconstitutional, fatally undermining the June 29 broader-rescheduling hearing; (3) the D.C. Circuit challenges could void the entire Schedule III framework. MMJ frames mainstream cannabis coverage as investor manipulation designed to support cannabis stock prices.
discourse.caution: MMJ International Holdings is a litigation party and a pharmaceutical competitor to state-licensed cannabis operators — its 'Fake Marijuana News' framing is itself advocacy, not independent analysis. The underlying legal challenges ARE real (the court has accepted the petitions; the ALJ constitutional issue is real; no stay has been granted as of June 19, 2026), but MMJ's framing exaggerates certainty of outcome to advance its own commercial interests. Oklahoma operators should evaluate D.C. Circuit developments through neutral legal counsel and primary court filings, not via MMJ International press releases. The order remains in effect and the registration deadline remains June 22 (dispensaries) and June 26–27 (manufacturers/cultivators).

source.name: AccessNewswire — MMJ International Holdings (June 13, 2026)

discourse.headline: 'The 280E Windfall May Leave Most Small Cannabis Businesses Behind' — compliance costs may outweigh tax savings for small operators
discourse.claim: WeedPress (May 31, 2026) argues that while large MSOs may benefit from 280E relief post-rescheduling, most small and mid-size cannabis operators will not realize the touted 'tax windfall' because: (1) DEA registration compliance costs (Section 11 cGMP requirements, legal fees for Section 4, $3,699/yr manufacturer fee) may exceed the actual tax savings for a small grow or processor; (2) DEA's six-month review period locks applicants in regulatory uncertainty; (3) the Section 4 self-incrimination exposure creates unavoidable legal costs; and (4) small operators who cannot meet Schedule III federal compliance expectations may face new federal exposure they did not have when operating in the pre-rescheduling legal gray zone.
discourse.caution: WeedPress is operated by an individual blogger with limited identified editorial credentials — it is not a peer-reviewed publication or established trade outlet. The underlying question (whether DEA registration compliance costs outweigh 280E savings for smaller operators) is legitimate and worth quantifying with your CPA and Oklahoma-licensed attorney. However, do NOT use this framing to conclude that DEA registration is optional for CAKE or other Oklahoma cultivators and processors — OBNDD's mandatory directive is not conditioned on whether the 280E tax benefit justifies the registration cost. The cost-benefit analysis is real; the conclusion that 'therefore don't register' is directly contradicted by OBNDD's enforcement position.

source.name: WeedPress — Many Operators Can't Comply With Federal Registration DEA Guidelines

discourse.headline: MMJ International features former DEA Section Chief Jorge Jimenez to oppose rescheduling — press release issued June 17, 2026
discourse.claim: MMJ International Holdings issued a press release on June 17, 2026 via AccessNewswire/Globe and Mail titled 'Former DEA Section Chief Steps Forward to Challenge Marijuana Rescheduling Process,' featuring Jorge Jimenez (described as a former DEA Section Chief) and other enforcement veterans arguing the April 28 rescheduling order is legally improper and the DEA ALJ framework underlying the June 29 hearing is unconstitutional. The release is the latest in MMJ's accelerating pre-hearing press campaign (releases issued May 18, May 26, June 3, June 12, June 13, and now June 17). It is being amplified on social media as evidence that the hearing and the entire rescheduling framework are about to collapse.
discourse.caution: This is a paid wire press release from MMJ International Holdings, a pharmaceutical cannabinoid company and formal litigation petitioner in D.C. Circuit Case 26-1136 — not independent journalism. A former DEA official's public opposition may carry rhetorical weight and potentially legal weight in the D.C. Circuit, but no court has acted on any of MMJ's filings and the June 29 hearing remains scheduled as of June 19, 2026 — with participant notifications sent June 18–19. The constitutional argument about DEA ALJ removal protections is real (DOJ conceded it in an earlier case), but it primarily affects the broader-rescheduling hearing — not the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order that is already in effect and governs OBNDD's mandatory registration requirement.

source.name: Globe and Mail / AccessNewswire — MMJ International (June 17, 2026)

discourse.headline: 'Wait for the dedicated manufacturer portal' — a critical false hope with 3 days remaining in the expedited window
discourse.claim: Multiple cannabis operators and some consultants have adopted a 'wait for the dedicated manufacturer portal' posture after DEA announced in May 2026 that cannabis-specific forms for manufacturers, bulk manufacturers (cultivators), analytical labs, and distributors were 'coming soon.' As of June 23, no such dedicated portal has launched. Some operators are still waiting, believing the portal will open before the June 26–27 deadline or that DEA will extend the window once the dedicated portal is live.
discourse.caution: The dedicated manufacturer portal has NOT launched and will not launch before the June 26–27 expedited window closes. DEA has given no public commitment on a launch date. The current filing pathway — DEA Form 225 with medical marijuana drug codes 7362/7353/7386 — is functional and confirmed by multiple law firms and Cannabis Business Times. DEA has explicitly stated operators who file via Form 225 before the dedicated portal launches do NOT need to reapply. With 3 days remaining as of June 23, any Oklahoma cultivator or processor who has not filed should use Form 225 immediately. OBNDD sanctions risk is real and the enforcement window is not conditional on the dedicated portal existing.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — DEA's Schedule III Cannabis Registration 'Coming Soon' for Cultivators, Manufacturers, Distributors, Labs

discourse.headline: Cannabis-industry attorney Jason Adelstone (Harris Sliwoski): D.C. Circuit challenge 'more likely than not' to stay and reverse rescheduling if standing established
discourse.claim: In a June 15–16, 2026 analysis distributed via AccessNewswire and the Canna Law Blog, Harris Sliwoski LLP attorney Jason Adelstone wrote that if the D.C. Circuit petitioners (NDASA, SAM, MMJ International, Nebraska, Indiana, and co-petitioners) establish standing and the court reaches the merits of the case, the DEA's marijuana rescheduling rule is 'more likely than not' to be stayed and ultimately reversed. Adelstone argued the petitioners' reliance on the D.C. Circuit's own precedent in NORML v. DEA may force the court to confront legal questions extending far beyond marijuana policy, and characterized the challenge as presenting a 'substantial threat' to the Schedule III rule.
discourse.caution: Harris Sliwoski is a reputable cannabis law firm and not an interested litigation party (unlike MMJ International), which lends this analysis more credibility than prior advocacy press releases. However, the key caveat is the 'IF petitioners establish standing' condition — standing is actively disputed and not guaranteed; the petitioners must first overcome standing hurdles before the court reaches the merits. Cannabis-industry attorneys have previously underestimated litigation risks in this space. More importantly for Oklahoma operators: this analysis does NOT change OBNDD's mandatory registration directive. The concrete risk of not registering by June 26–27 (OBNDD sanctions, potential license jeopardy) remains immediate and certain, while the stay risk — though real and now conceded by a cannabis-friendly attorney — has not materialized. Do NOT use this analysis as a basis to delay or forego DEA registration. Consult Oklahoma-licensed counsel who can assess both the OBNDD mandate and the litigation risk simultaneously.

source.name: Globe and Mail / AccessNewswire — Harris Sliwoski LLP (June 16, 2026)

discourse.headline: 'Rigged hearing' narrative: cannabis industry and advocates describe DEA participant selection as a structured all-opposition proceeding
discourse.claim: Following DEA's release of the June 29 hearing participant list (~June 18–19, 2026) — which includes only rescheduling opponents — cannabis trade groups, patient advocates, and reform organizations are describing the hearing as structurally designed to produce an anti-rescheduling administrative record. Commentary on social media and cannabis trade outlets frames the all-opponent selection as 'rigged,' argues it delegitimizes the process, and suggests it will strengthen D.C. Circuit petitioners' argument that the proceeding is constitutionally or procedurally defective. NORML — which was excluded — had filed a notice of intent to participate specifically arguing that cannabis consumers must be represented.
discourse.caution: The participant selection is a confirmed fact, not merely a claim. The 'rigged' characterization is commentary — no court has found the selection process improper as of June 20, 2026. The broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing addresses whether adult-use marijuana moves to Schedule III — it is procedurally and legally separate from the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order already in effect. Oklahoma cultivators and processors must still use Form 225 to meet the June 26–27 expedited window. Do not allow commentary about the hearing's participant composition to create a false impression that the entire rescheduling framework is collapsing — no stay has been granted as of June 20, 2026, and OBNDD's mandatory registration directive is unchanged.

source.name: MJBizDaily — DEA Selects Only Cannabis Foes for Hearing

discourse.headline: 'They're already at the door' — DEA inspection reports renew operator fears that registration triggers immediate federal on-site presence
discourse.claim: Following Marijuana Moment's June 19 report that DEA has begun on-site inspections at cannabis businesses that applied for Schedule III registration, operators are sharing reactions across industry forums and social media. The predominant narrative: registration has already triggered 5–6 federal agents arriving for six-hour site visits, confirming that applying is not a paper exercise but a direct invitation for DEA to map your facility, operations, and personnel. Operators who were holding off — particularly cultivators waiting for the manufacturer portal — are pointing to the Mississippi inspection reports as validation of the existing 'giving DEA a roadmap' concern. Some social commentary describes the Mississippi visits as a 'dry run' before DEA scales inspections nationally and warns that 'every dispensary that filed is getting a visit.'
discourse.caution: The Mississippi inspections reported by Marijuana Moment (June 19, 2026) are consistent with DEA's standard Schedule III registration review process — inspections are a normal and expected step, not a surprise enforcement action. The collaborative, inquisitive tone described by High Street Dispensary owner David Fowler does not support the interpretation that DEA is using inspections as pretextual enforcement. More critically for Oklahoma cultivators and processors: OBNDD Director Anderson has issued a mandatory directive to register — the risk of NOT registering by June 26–27 (OBNDD sanctions, potential OMMA license revocation) is immediate and concrete. Using inspection news as a reason to avoid filing directly contradicts OBNDD's mandate and does not eliminate the underlying compliance obligation. Operators genuinely concerned about inspection readiness should consult counsel and prepare documentation now — then file, not skip.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Begins On-Site Inspections (June 19, 2026)
